perturbation.original_text: Shareholder will, during the Restrictive Period, within ten days after accepting any employment, consulting engagement, engagement as an independent contractor, partnership or other association that might reasonably involve the Business, advise Heritage of the identity of the new employer, client, partner or other Person with whom Shareholder has become associated.
perturbation.changed_text: Shareholder will, during the Restrictive Period, within one day after accepting any employment, consulting engagement, engagement as an independent contractor, partnership or other association that might reasonably involve the Business, advise Heritage of the identity of the new employer, client, partner or other Person with whom Shareholder has become associated.
perturbation.explanation: Shortening the time frame for advising Heritage of new employment to one day creates a compliance issue, as it may be unreasonably short and conflict with the California labor laws regarding employee notification periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 2802 requires employers to indemnify employees for all necessary expenditures or losses incurred as a direct consequence of the employee’s duties. Requiring notification within one day may not provide sufficient time for the employee to gather necessary information or seek legal counsel, potentially leading to expenses for which the employee should be indemnified, but may not be if the employee is rushed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 2802 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 3. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 2. Obligations of Employer [2800 - 2810.8] ( Article 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 2802. (a) An employer shall indemnify his or her employee for all necessary expenditures or losses incurred by the employee in direct consequence of the discharge of his or her duties, or of his or her obedience to the directions of the employer, even though unlawful, unless the employee, at the time of obeying the directions, believed them to be unlawful. (b) All awards made by a court or by the Division of Labor Standards Enforcement for reimbursement of necessary expenditures under this section shall carry interest at the same rate as judgments in civil actions. Interest shall accrue from the date on which the employee incurred the necessary expenditure or loss. (c) For purposes of this section, the term “necessary expenditures or losses” shall include all reasonable costs, including, but not limited to, attorney’s fees incurred by the employee enforcing the rights granted by this section. (d) In addition to recovery of penalties under this section in a court action or proceedi
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notification timeframe to one day, which the Law Explanation argues may not allow sufficient time for the employee to gather information or seek legal counsel, potentially leading to expenses. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Labor Code § 2802(a), states that an employer shall indemnify his or her employee for all necessary expenditures or losses incurred by the employee in direct consequence of the discharge of his or her duties, or of his or her obedience to the directions of the employer. The Perturbation Explanation also suggests that a one-day notification period may be unreasonably short and create compliance issues under California labor laws. The inconsistency between the original contract's ten-day period and the changed contract's one-day period creates a direct contradiction with the intent of the law, as it places an undue burden on the employee, potentially leading to expenses they should be indemnified for.

perturbation.original_text: Shareholder hereby agrees not to, at any time, disclose to any unauthorized Persons or use for his or its own account or for the benefit of any third party any Confidential Information, whether or not such information is 2 embodied in writing or other physical form or is retained in the memory of Shareholder, without Heritage’s written consent, unless and to the extent that the Confidential Information is or becomes generally known to and available for use by the public other than as a result of Shareholder’s fault or the fault of any other Person bound by a duty of confidentiality to Heritage, HBC or the Affiliated Companies.
perturbation.changed_text: Shareholder hereby agrees not to, at any time within 6 months, disclose to any unauthorized Persons or use for his or its own account or for the benefit of any third party any Confidential Information, whether or not such information is 2 embodied in writing or other physical form or is retained in the memory of Shareholder, without Heritage’s written consent, unless and to the extent that the Confidential Information is or becomes generally known to and available for use by the public other than as a result of Shareholder’s fault or the fault of any other Person bound by a duty of confidentiality to Heritage, HBC or the Affiliated Companies.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the confidentiality agreement to 6 months may be deemed unreasonable and thus unenforceable under California law, which requires narrowly tailored confidentiality agreements to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires that confidentiality agreements be reasonable in duration and scope. A 6-month limit may be deemed insufficient to protect the company's legitimate trade secrets, especially considering the executive's access to sensitive information. This limited duration could be viewed as a violation of the act if it doesn't adequately protect the trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a time limit (6 months) on the confidentiality agreement, which contradicts the purpose of trade secret protection. The Law Explanation states that California law requires confidentiality agreements to be 'reasonable in duration,' implying that a 6-month limit may be insufficient to protect trade secrets. Snippet 1, referencing the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CIV 3426), establishes the legal basis for trade secret protection, suggesting that the limited duration could violate the act if it fails to adequately protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Shareholder hereby covenants and agrees that from the Effective Date until the third (3 rd ) anniversary of the Effective Date (“Restricted Period”), Shareholder will not without the prior written consent of Heritage, engage or participate or have any interest, directly or indirectly, in any Business anywhere in the counties of Santa Clara, Alameda, Contra Costa, Marin, San Francisco and San Mateo located in the State of California (“Restricted Territory”)
perturbation.changed_text: Shareholder hereby covenants and agrees that from the Effective Date until the fifth (5th) anniversary of the Effective Date (“Restricted Period”), Shareholder will not without the prior written consent of Heritage, engage or participate or have any interest, directly or indirectly, in any Business anywhere in the counties of Santa Clara, Alameda, Contra Costa, Marin, San Francisco and San Mateo located in the State of California (“Restricted Territory”)
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-compete period to five years raises concerns about enforceability under California law, which generally disfavors non-compete agreements and requires them to be narrowly tailored to protect trade secrets. A five-year period may be deemed unreasonably long.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While there are narrow exceptions, such as for the sale of a business, a five-year non-compete agreement is more likely to be deemed an unreasonable restraint on trade, especially if it is not strictly necessary to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends a non-compete agreement to five years, while the original text had it at three years. The Perturbation Explanation raises concerns that a five-year term may be unreasonably long under California law. The Law Explanation and Scraped Law Snippet 1 both cite California Business and Professions Code § 16600, which generally prohibits contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession. Snippet 1 specifically states that the section should be read broadly to void the application of non-compete agreements. Therefore, extending the non-compete period to five years creates a significant risk of violating California law by creating an unreasonable restraint on trade, making the terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Therefore, Shareholder hereby agrees not to, at any time, disclose to any unauthorized Persons or use for his or its own account or for the benefit of any third party any Confidential Information, whether or not such information is 2 embodied in writing or other physical form or is retained in the memory of Shareholder, without Heritage’s written consent, unless and to the extent that the Confidential Information is or becomes generally known to and available for use by the public other than as a result of Shareholder’s fault or the fault of any other Person bound by a duty of confidentiality to Heritage, HBC or the Affiliated Companies.
perturbation.changed_text: Therefore, Shareholder hereby agrees not to, at any time, disclose to any unauthorized Persons or use for his or its own account or for the benefit of any third party any Confidential Information, whether or not such information is 2 embodied in writing or other physical form or is retained in the memory of Shareholder, without Heritage’s written consent, unless and to the extent that the Confidential Information is or becomes generally known to and available for use by the public.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'other than as a result of Shareholder’s fault or the fault of any other Person bound by a duty of confidentiality to Heritage, HBC or the Affiliated Companies' means that even if the information becomes public due to the fault of Heritage, the shareholder is still bound by confidentiality. This is an unreasonable restriction.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1670.5 addresses unconscionable contracts. A clause that holds the Shareholder liable for maintaining confidentiality even if Heritage makes the information public is potentially unconscionable, as it creates an undue burden on the Shareholder and shields Heritage from the consequences of its own actions. The court can refuse to enforce the unconscionable clause, or the entire contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes a critical exception to the confidentiality agreement, creating an unconscionable burden on the Shareholder. The original text allowed for disclosure if the information became public through no fault of the Shareholder. The changed text removes this protection, potentially holding the Shareholder liable even if Heritage was the cause of the information becoming public. This is potentially unconscionable and therefore contradicts the provision of California Civil Code § 1670.5, as cited in the Law Explanation, which allows a court to refuse to enforce a contract or clause deemed unconscionable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that the court can refuse to enforce the contract or the unconscionable clause.
